title: Henry Ford and the Ethics of Number Go Down
body_html:
Henry Ford is one of the essay’s hero examples because he understood that lowering prices was not charity and not sabotage. It was strategy. By relentlessly reducing the cost of the automobile, Ford expanded the market, increased turnover, and widened the social base of industrial modernity.

What makes this important is not just business history but business ethics. Ford’s framing treated service to the customer as the path to sustainable profit rather than the enemy of it. Lower prices were not a sacrifice demanded by regulators or moralists; they were what a competent industrialist produced when process, scale, and engineering improved.

That stands in sharp contrast to the fiat worldview, which often treats the consumer almost as a macroeconomic input to be manipulated. In Ford’s world, the producer earns legitimacy by making previously scarce goods broadly accessible. In the fiat world, policymakers too often defend structures that preserve nominal flows even when they erode real affordability.

Ford embodies the essay’s title in practice. Number go down is not an apocalyptic outcome. In a healthy system it is one of the cleanest ways business proves that it is serving society.
